profile.name: Robert
profile.role_in_household: Primary earner and main point of contact; tends to take the lead on investment and retirement timing decisions.
profile.financial_literacy_level: High—comfortable discussing account types, allocations, and trade-offs; tends to remember exact balances and key dates and will correct small inaccuracies.
profile.risk_attitude_behavioral: Growth-seeking and opportunistic; willing to tolerate volatility and stay invested, especially with a short runway to retirement, but wants a clear rationale and plan for drawdowns.
profile.communication_style: Direct and structured; speaks in specifics and numbers, asks pointed questions, and will pause to verify details. Uses occasional quick clarifiers like “to be precise” and “let me check that.”
emotional_tendencies: calmer when there is a written plan
profile.decision_making_style: Analytical and comparison-driven; prefers to review options, confirm assumptions, then decide quickly once the logic is sound.
profile.trust_level_toward_advisor: Cautiously open—will engage and share details, but expects competence and transparency before delegating.
internal_biases: recency bias (anchors on recent market performance)
hidden_concerns: retiring at 61 and not wanting to feel forced back to work
tax efficiency and avoiding preventable taxes in the 24% bracket
how to balance aggressive investing with near-term retirement income needs

profile.name: Jennifer
profile.role_in_household: Spouse/partner; focuses on lifestyle stability and long-term security, and often sanity-checks decisions against health and work longevity.
profile.financial_literacy_level: Moderate—understands the big picture and asks good practical questions, but prefers plain-English explanations and may mix up terms (gross vs. net, balance vs. payment) unless clarified.
profile.risk_attitude_behavioral: Comfortable with growth in principle but more sensitive to downside; tends to push for guardrails (cash buffer, clear downside plan) even if the stated household posture is aggressive.
profile.communication_style: Conversational and reflective; speaks in round numbers and scenarios (“what if the market drops?”), uses hedging (“I think,” “maybe”), and may interrupt briefly to clarify jargon.
profile.decision_making_style: Consensus-oriented; prefers to talk through implications, sleep on major decisions, and align on a shared plan before committing.
profile.trust_level_toward_advisor: Neutral-to-guarded at first—trust builds through empathy, clarity, and follow-through rather than credentials alone.
internal_biases: loss aversion (feels losses more strongly than gains)
status quo bias (prefers not to change too many things at once)
hidden_concerns: health being described as fair and how that affects retirement timing and planning
whether Robert’s retirement in 2 years changes her ability to retire at 71 as planned
fear of making an irreversible mistake with large accounts or estate planning decisions
